concepts.title: Stationary Wave
concepts.conceptBody:
Stationary waves in Strings 

When a string capable of vibrating (under tension) is set into vibration, transverse harmonic waves will propagate along it. It gets reflected at the other fixed end. The incident and the reflected waves interfere to produce a stationary transverse wave in which the ends are always nodes, if both ends are fixed, whereas if there is a free end and one fixed end, then free end will be an antinode as here displacement will be maximum.

concepts.title: Fundamental Modes of Stationary Waves
concepts.conceptBody:
Fundamental Modes of Stationary Waves 

In the simplest form, the string vibrates in one loop in which the ends are the nodes and the centre is the antinode. This mode of vibration is known as the fundamental mode and frequency of vibration is known as the fundamental frequency or first harmonic.

 

Since the distance between consecutive nodes is

$\text{L}=\frac{{{\lambda }_{1}}}{2}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\therefore {{\lambda }_{1}}=2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}$

If ${{f}_{1}}$ is the fundamental frequency of vibration, then the velocity of transverse waves is given as, $\text{v}={{\lambda }_{1}}{{\text{f}}_{1}}$

Or ${{\text{f}}_{1}}=\frac{\text{v}}{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}}$ … (i)

concepts.title: Frequencies of Stationary Waves
concepts.conceptBody:
Modes of Stationary Waves 

The same string under the same conditions may also vibrate in two loops, such that the centre is also the node

 

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}=2{{\lambda }_{2}}/2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\therefore {{\lambda }_{2}}=\text{L}$

If ${{\text{f}}_{2}}$ is frequency of vibrations

$\therefore $ ${{\text{f}}_{2}}=\frac{\text{v}}{{{\lambda }_{2}}}=\frac{\text{v}}{\text{L}}$

$\therefore $ ${{\text{f}}_{2}}=\frac{\text{v}}{\text{L}}$ … (ii)

The frequency ${{f}_{2}}$ is known as second harmonic or first overcome. 

The same string under the same conditions may also vibrate in three segments.

 

$\therefore $ $\text{L}=\frac{3{{\lambda }_{3}}}{2}$

$\therefore $ ${{\lambda }_{3}}=\frac{2}{3}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}$

If ${{\text{f}}_{3}}$ is the frequency in this mode of vibration, then,

${{\text{f}}_{3}}=\frac{3\text{v}}{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}}$ … (iii)

The frequency ${{f}_{3}}$ is known as third harmonic or second overtone. 

Thus a stretched string vibrates with frequencies, which are integral multiples of the fundamental frequencies. These frequencies are known as harmonics.

The velocity of transverse wave in stretched string is given as $\text{v}=\sqrt{\frac{\text{T}}{\mu }}$. Where $\text{T}=$ tension in the string.

$\mu =$ linear density or mass per unit length of string. If the string fixed at two ends, vibrates in its fundamental mode, then $\text{v}=2\text{Lf}$

$\text{f}=\frac{1}{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}}\sqrt{\frac{\text{T}}{\mu }}$

Now as in case of waves $\text{v}=\text{f}\lambda $, i.e., $\text{f}=\text{v}/\lambda $ and for waves along a string $\text{v}=\sqrt{\left( \text{T}/\text{m} \right)}$, the possible frequencies of vibration of the string in the light of Eqn. (1) will be :

${{\text{f}}_{\text{n}}}=\frac{\text{n}}{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}}\sqrt{\frac{\text{T}}{\text{m}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ with }\!\!~\!\!\text{ n}=1,2,3,\ldots $

i.e., in case of vibrations of strings, number of natural frequencies are possible and if we take

$\begin{matrix}
\text{f}=\frac{1}{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}}\sqrt{\frac{\text{T}}{\text{m}}},{{\text{f}}_{\text{n}}}=\text{nf} \\
\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{f}}_{1}}=\text{f},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{f}}_{2}}=2\text{f},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{f}}_{3}}=3\text{f},\ldots \\
\end{matrix}$

So in case of vibrations of strings higher frequencies are integral multiples of $f$, i.e., forms a harmonic series.

The frequency will be minimum when $\text{n}=\text{min}=1$, i.e.

${{f}_{\text{min}}}={{f}_{1}}=f=\frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}$

Regarding frequency of a vibrating string it is worth noting that :

(1) As a string has many natural frequencies (all integral multiples of fundamental frequency), so when it is excited with a tuning fork (or a vibrating body), the string will be in resonance with the given body if any one of its natural frequencies coincides with that of the body.

(2) As for a sting $\text{f}=\left( 1/2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L} \right)\left( \sqrt{\text{T}/\text{m}} \right)$ 
(a) $f\propto \left( 1/L \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ if $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }T$ and $m$ are constant 
(b) $\text{f}\propto \sqrt{\text{T}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ if $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}$ and $\text{m}$ are constant 
(c) $\text{f}\propto \left( 1/\sqrt{\text{m}} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ if $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}$ and $\text{L}$ are constant

These laws of vibration of string are known as Mersenne's laws of vibration of string and according to these the frequency of a string can be changed by changing its length, tension or mass per unit length.

(3) If $\text{M}$ is the mass of a string of length $\text{L},\text{m}=\left( \text{M}/\text{L} \right)$

So $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}=\frac{1}{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}}\sqrt{\frac{\text{T}}{\text{m}}}=\frac{1}{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}}\sqrt{\frac{\text{T}}{\left( \text{M}/\text{L} \right)}}=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\text{T}}{\text{ML}}}$ … (4)

Also if the radius of string is $r$ and its density $\rho ,m=\pi {{r}^{2}}\rho $.

So $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}=\frac{1}{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}}\sqrt{\frac{\text{T}}{\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}}\rho }}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ i.e., $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}=\frac{1}{2\text{Lr}}\sqrt{\frac{\text{T}}{\pi \rho }}$ … (5)

(4) If the string is vibrating in nth harmonic, its frequency will be nf, the number of loops in the string or antinodes will be $\text{n}$, while total number of nodes (including two at the ends) will be $\left( n+1 \right)$, e.g., in case of 3rd harmonic of a vibrating string, frequency of vibration will be 3 f, antinodes will be 3 while total nodes $=3+1=4$ and $\lambda =\left( 2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}/3 \right)$.

(5) In case of vibrations of composite string (i.e., string made up by joining two strings of different lengths, cross-sections and densities) having same tension throughout, the joint is a node while lowest common fundamental frequency of the string will be

${{\text{f}}_{\text{C}}}={{\text{n}}_{1}}{{\text{f}}_{1}}={{\text{n}}_{2}}{{\text{f}}_{2}}$

Here higher harmonics will be integral multiples of common frequency ${{f}_{C}}$.
